treatment: Spray Chlorothalonil 75% WP @ 2 g/L or Mancozeb 75% WP @ 2.5 g/L every 7 to 10 days.
Remove lower infected leaves and keep foliage dry after irrigation.
fertilizer: Reduce excess nitrogen to avoid soft lush growth that favors fungal spread.
Apply balanced NPK and calcium to support foliage recovery.
prevention: Follow 2 to 3 year crop rotation away from tomato and potato.
Use drip irrigation and prune for airflow.

treatment: Start protective Mancozeb spray immediately and rotate with Cymoxanil + Mancozeb where locally approved.
Destroy heavily infected plant parts to slow spread.
fertilizer: Avoid late heavy nitrogen doses during cool humid periods.
Support plants with potassium-rich foliar nutrition if leaf burn is limited.
prevention: Avoid overhead irrigation and keep field drainage clear.
Increase scouting during foggy or rainy weeks.

treatment: Rogue severely infected plants early and control whiteflies with neem oil or locally approved insecticide.
Use yellow sticky traps around the field.
fertilizer: Maintain moderate nitrogen and add micronutrients through a balanced foliar spray.
Avoid excessive urea because it increases tender growth preferred by vectors.
prevention: Use vector-proof nursery nets and reflective mulch where possible.
Keep alternate host weeds removed from field borders.

treatment: No curative spray is needed right now; continue weekly scouting.
fertilizer: Continue balanced fertigation based on crop stage and soil test.
prevention: Keep canopy aerated and sanitize pruning tools between plots.

treatment: Spray Tricyclazole 75% WP @ 0.6 g/L when first spindle lesions appear.
Avoid excess nitrogen top dressing during humid weeks.
fertilizer: Split nitrogen into smaller doses and increase potassium where deficiency is visible.
Do not over-fertilize lush fields.
prevention: Use tolerant varieties and maintain recommended spacing.
Drain standing water temporarily if the canopy stays wet for long periods.

treatment: Apply validamycin or a locally recommended fungicide when lesions move above lower sheaths.
Remove dense lodged tillers in small plots.
fertilizer: Cut back on excess nitrogen and keep potassium adequate.
Support recovery with balanced basal nutrition.
prevention: Avoid overly dense transplanting and keep the field well ventilated.
Use clean crop residue management after harvest.

treatment: Drain excess water and avoid field operations when leaves are wet.
Use a copper-based bactericide only if recommended by the local extension officer.
fertilizer: Reduce nitrogen pressure and increase potash support.
Do not stimulate new soft growth during active infection.
prevention: Use healthy seed, avoid clipping seedlings harshly, and maintain clean irrigation channels.
Scout after storms and heavy winds.

treatment: Spray Propiconazole or Tebuconazole according to local guidance after pustules appear.
Protect the flag leaf first in high-value fields.
fertilizer: Keep nitrogen moderate and maintain balanced sulphur nutrition.
Avoid late heavy nitrogen if disease is active.
prevention: Use rust-tolerant varieties and remove volunteer wheat.
Increase scouting during cool humid weather.

treatment: Apply wettable sulphur or a recommended fungicide early in infection.
Improve airflow by avoiding excessive canopy density.
fertilizer: Keep nitrogen balanced and avoid unnecessary foliar nitrogen.
Add potash if lodging risk and disease pressure rise together.
prevention: Prefer tolerant varieties and timely sowing.
Monitor field edges and shaded patches first.

treatment: There is no strong curative field treatment after grain infection; focus on seed quality and timing.
Consult local extension support if seed production is the target.
fertilizer: Maintain balanced nutrition and avoid late irrigation around flowering.
Do not push late nitrogen near heading.
prevention: Use certified seed and follow clean seed handling.
Avoid sowing schedules that place flowering in persistent humid windows.

treatment: Control whiteflies quickly using neem-based products or locally approved chemistry.
Remove heavily infected plants to reduce inoculum.
fertilizer: Avoid excess nitrogen and support plants with balanced NPK plus magnesium if needed.
Keep growth steady instead of overly vegetative.
prevention: Manage weeds and alternate hosts around the field.
Avoid overlapping cotton crops that keep whitefly populations high.

treatment: Improve drainage and protect bolls with a recommended fungicide if weather remains wet.
Remove rotting bolls during scouting rounds.
fertilizer: Balance potassium to improve boll strength and avoid excess nitrogen.
Use foliar potassium where deficiency is visible.
prevention: Maintain open canopy architecture and insect control.
Prevent standing water in furrows.

treatment: Spray Mancozeb or copper fungicide where lesions are increasing.
Remove crop debris that carries inoculum.
fertilizer: Maintain balanced NPK and avoid stress from irregular irrigation.
Add micronutrients if leaf tissue shows deficiency symptoms.
prevention: Use clean field sanitation and avoid prolonged leaf wetness.
Rotate away from heavily infested fields.

treatment: Protect upper leaves with a recommended fungicide when lesions are active before tasseling.
Remove severely infected leaf trash after harvest.
fertilizer: Keep potassium adequate and avoid stressing plants with uneven nitrogen.
Support recovery with balanced top dressing after rainfall.
prevention: Choose tolerant hybrids and rotate residue-heavy fields.
Increase scouting after long dew periods.

treatment: Rogue systemic infections early and use seed-treatment-centered management for future lots.
Improve field drainage immediately.
fertilizer: Avoid heavy early nitrogen in waterlogged fields.
Keep phosphorus adequate for early vigor.
prevention: Use treated seed and avoid repeated maize in wet low-lying plots.
Manage volunteer maize and alternate grass hosts.

treatment: Apply a protective fungicide when elongated lesions spread quickly.
Keep lower leaves monitored after rain.
fertilizer: Maintain balanced nitrogen and potassium.
Prevent moisture stress during tasseling.
prevention: Use residue management and crop rotation.
Prefer tolerant hybrids for historically affected areas.

treatment: Confirm the diagnosis with a local agronomist before using curative chemicals.
Remove heavily infected leaves and keep field tools sanitized.
fertilizer: Avoid excess nitrogen during active disease pressure.
Use balanced nutrition guided by crop stage and soil test.
prevention: Improve drainage, airflow, and scouting frequency.
Keep crop residue and weeds managed around the plot.
